variants.llm_generated: In the United States, you are legally required to report and pay taxes on all income, including money earned through Patreon, regardless of the amount. However, Patreon is only required to issue a 1099-K tax form if you earn more than $20,000 and have over 200 transactions in a calendar year. Even if you do not receive a 1099-K, you are still responsible for reporting your Patreon income to the IRS. Depending on whether your Patreon activity is considered a business or a hobby, you would report this income on Schedule C or as 'other income' on your tax return. If your Patreon earnings are significant, you may also need to make quarterly estimated tax payments to avoid penalties.
variants.llm_humanized: So, in the US, technically you have to report any income you make, even from Patreon, no matter how small. But Patreon itself only sends you a 1099-K if you make over $20,000 and have more than 200 transactions in a year. That means if you make less than that, you probably won’t get a tax form from them, but you’re still supposed to report it on your taxes. How you report it depends on whether you treat your Patreon as a business or just a hobby—so you’d use Schedule C or just put it as 'other income.' And if you’re making a lot, you might need to pay estimated taxes during the year to avoid any penalties.
variants.human_polished: If you are in the US, you are legally required to file taxes on any income, including money earned from Patreon, regardless of the amount. The amount of tax you owe depends on your total taxable income. For small transactions, payments are often not reported to the IRS, so some people do not file or pay taxes on them. Patreon only issues a 1099-K form if you earn more than $20,000 in a calendar year and have over 200 transactions. If Patreon does not report your income, the IRS may not be aware of it unless you are audited. However, you are still legally responsible for reporting all income, whether or not the IRS receives a report. To report your Patreon income, you would use Schedule C if it is a business, or add it to 'other income' on your 1040 if it is a hobby. If you earn a significant amount, you should consider making quarterly estimated tax payments using Form 1040-ES to avoid penalties for underpayment.
variants.dictated_spoken: Okay so if you’re in the US, you’re supposed to report any income you make, even if it’s just a little bit from Patreon. Now, how much tax you actually pay depends on your total income for the year. For small amounts, a lot of times the IRS doesn’t get notified because Patreon only sends a 1099 if you make over $20,000 in a year and have more than 200 transactions. So if you’re under that, they probably won’t report it, and the IRS might not know unless you get audited or something. But technically, you’re still supposed to report it yourself. When you do your taxes, you’d either use Schedule C if it’s a business, or just put it as other income on your 1040 if it’s more of a hobby. And if you’re making a lot, you might want to send in quarterly payments so you don’t get hit with a penalty for not withholding enough.
